content.title: Cutting Edge [C0029]
content.summary:
In early 2024, Volexity disclosed active, "in the wild" exploitation of two critical zero-day vulnerabilities (CVE-2023-46805 and CVE-2024-21887) in Ivanti Connect Secure VPN appliances; this exploit chain allowed attackers to bypass authentication and achieve remote code execution. The initial exploitation has been attributed to China-nexus espionage actors, identified as UNC5221/UTA0178 and UNC5325.

The attackers gained persistent access to the devices, implanted webshells, stole credentials, and moved laterally inside networks while relying on living-off-the-land techniques to blend in with legitimate administrator activity. Despite vendor mitigations released quickly after disclosure, exploitation continued and scaled up, affecting more than 1,700 devices worldwide and involving more attackers than originally identified.

This incident highlights how compromise of perimeter trust anchors can collapse the network trust boundary and enable credential theft, persistence, and follow-on intrusion activity.
content.incident_overview:
It is not known exactly when the vulnerabilities were first discovered or exploited, but the earliest confirmed exploitation activity identified by Volexity on a customer network took place on December 3, 2023. Throughout the rest of the month, the threat actor used the exploit chain to compromise Ivanti Connect Secure VPN devices, modify files, disable logs, and maintain access.

On January 10, 2024, Volexity publicly reported the exploitation activity and Ivanti published mitigations and advisories. By that stage, the activity had already expanded well beyond the first identified victims. Volexity later reported more than 1,700 compromised devices worldwide and the involvement of additional adversaries. Proof-of-concept code became public on January 16, 2024, likely accelerating opportunistic exploitation. Additional exploitation waves continued from early 2024 into 2025.

The exploitation generally followed these steps:

- Reconnaissance for vulnerable devices and target selection
- Initial Access via chaining the two vulnerabilities, achieving remote code execution
- Post exploit actions included stealing credentials, installing backdoors and disabling integrity controls
- With the use of stolen credentials further lateral movement took place
- Attackers would evade defenses by tampering with logging and integrity tools
- In the end attackers could use their access to discover high-value assets and potentially exfiltrate data
content.adversary_objectives: The most likely objective was to establish long-term access and persistence for espionage and intelligence collection; more specific objectives depended on the particular threat actor and target.

techniques: Persistence: Web Shell [T1505.003], Compromise Host Software Binary [T1554]
Defense Evasion: Disable or Modify Tools [T1562.001], File Deletion [T1070.004], Timestomp [T1070.006], Encrypted/Encoded File [T1027.013]
Credential Access: Keylogging [T1056.001], Web Portal Capture [T1056.003], LSASS Memory [T1003.001], NTDS [T1003.003]
Discovery: System Information Discovery [T1082], Search Victim-Owned Websites [T1594]
Lateral Movement: Remote Desktop Protocol [T1021.001], SMB / Windows Admin Shares [T1021.002], SSH [T1021.004], Domain Accounts [T1078.002]
Command and Control: DNS [T1071.004], Protocol Tunneling [T1572], Non-Application Layer Protocol [T1095], Traffic Signaling [T1205]
procedure_examples: Cutting Edge began with Vulnerability Scanning [T1595.002], where threat actors identified appliances vulnerable to CVE-2023-46805, CVE-2024-21887, and later CVE-2024-21893 which were then exploited using Exploit Public-Facing Application [T1190].
Following exploitation, attackers used Command and Scripting Interpreter [T1059], Python [T1059.006] to deploy tooling, enumerate host data, and establish interactive access.
Persistence was achieved through the deployment of Server Software Component: Web Shell [T1505.003].

To evade detection, threat actors made use of Compromise Host Software Binary [T1554] and Impair Defenses: Disable or Modify Tools [T1562.001]. Additional Defense Evasion included Indicator Removal [T1070], File Deletion [T1070.004] and Timestomp [T1070.006].

Credential Access was performed by modifying JavaScript loaded by the Ivanti Web SSL VPN login page to capture usernames and passwords, in other words Input Capture: Web Portal Capture [T1056.003] and Input Capture: Keylogging [T1056.001].
Stolen credentials were reused as Valid Accounts: Domain Accounts [T1078.002] to move laterally via Remote Services: RDP [T1021.001], SMB/Windows Admin Shares [T1021.002] and SSH [T1021.004].
Attackers expanded credential access by OS Credential Dumping: LSASS Memory [T1003.001] and NTDS [T1003.003].
Internal Discovery was conducted by System Information Discovery [T1082].
For Command and Control, the campaign relied on Application Layer Protocol: DNS [T1071.004], Protocol Tunneling [T1572] and Non-Application Layer Protocol [T1095].

Archive Collected Data: Archive via Utility [T1560.001] took place in the form of .tar files prior to exfiltration, while Compromise Infrastructure: Network Devices [T1584.008] was used to repurpose compromised network devices as C2 infrastructure to blend with legitimate network traffic.

content.defensive_analysis:
The main defensive failure in the Cutting Edge campaign came from the multiple critical vulnerabilities in Ivanti VPN appliances, these components acted as perimeter trust anchors for affected organizations. The existence of vulnerabilities in complex software is unavoidable, but the combination of authentication bypass, command injection and SSRF flaws in an internet-facing device resulted in a disproportionately high impact, basically collapsing the network trust boundary. A factor that greatly contributed to the severity of the incident was the implicit trust put in network infrastructure. The use of VPNs is itself a good and widely accepted security practice; however, these devices were often treated as inherently trustworthy and were not as scrutinized as endpoints.

Another important point is that the campaign did not rely heavily on traditional malware. Attackers abused legitimate binaries, modified trusted components, and deployed lightweight web shells, which helped them maintain access while reducing reliance on easily detected malware. Disclosure and mitigation were also difficult: early mitigations and vendor guidance proved insufficient, and the discovery of an additional vulnerability allowed attackers to bypass some protections. Public awareness then helped shift the campaign from targeted exploitation to broader opportunistic activity.

Despite the failures, the incident showed significant successes. Volexity’s early detection and investigation were instrumental in uncovering the exploit chain and scope of compromise. Their work enabled coordinated disclosure with Ivanti, timely government advisories, and rapid dissemination of IoCs.

In the end, Cutting Edge shows that the most critical failures were rooted in assumptions about trust, visibility, and detection at the network perimeter. But it also highlights how effective threat research and coordinated response can limit the impact of even highly sophisticated intrusion campaigns.
lessons_learned: Network infrastructure devices must be treated as part of the attack surface, else they can become highly effective initial access vectors.

Security-critical software should be designed and implemented with defense-in-depth principles from the ground up plus general best practices for secure coding. Quietly discovering and fixing vulnerabilities before deployment is often the most effective defense.
Vendor mitigations and configuration workarounds should not be mistaken for full remediation.
Detection strategies must not rely only on malware signatures; attacks that make use of legitimate components and “live off the system” can evade signature-based detection.

Edge-device compromises undermine traditional assumptions about “external” versus “internal” attackers and once the perimeter is breached, lateral movement can appear legitimate, emphasizing the need for continuous verification.
File and configuration integrity monitoring is as important as malware detection as attackers can alter defensive controls.
Early detection, timely investigation and responsible disclosure, significantly reduce dwell time and improve community-wide defensive readiness.
Public disclosure is a double-edged sword. While it enables defenders to respond, it can also accelerate opportunistic exploitation, so organizations must prepare for rapid attacker adaptation.
content.reflection:
This case reinforces the importance of secure design, secure deployment, and careful scrutiny of perimeter systems. The most useful takeaway is how quickly trust can collapse when an internet-facing appliance becomes the intrusion point.

It also shows how pragmatic attackers can be. In this campaign, the value came not from noisy malware but from chaining vulnerabilities, modifying trusted components, and using legitimate tools to evade detection and maintain persistence.

content.defensive_mapping:
_Scope note: This is a non-exhaustive, conceptual mapping of defensive approaches to observed techniques, not a prescriptive checklist._

- Exploit Public-Facing Application [T1190]

  - Hardening: Segment Address Offset Randomization, Process Segment Execution Prevention
  - Detection: Protocol Metadata Anomaly Detection, Remote Terminal Session Detection, Network Traffic Signature Analysis, Inbound Session Volume Analysis, Client-server Payload Profiling, Per Host Download-Upload Ratio Analysis, Network Traffic Community Deviation, Database Query String Analysis
  - Isolation: Network Traffic Filtering, Inbound Traffic Filtering

- Command and Scripting Interpreter [T1059]

  - Hardening: File Encryption
  - Detection: Dynamic Analysis, Emulated File Analysis, File Integrity Monitoring, File Analysis
  - Isolation: Content Modification, Content Quarantine, Executable Allowlisting, Executable Denylisting, Local File Permissions, Content Filtering, Remote File Access Mediation
  - Deception: Decoy File
  - Eviction: File Eviction
  - Restoration: Restore File

- Web Shell [T1505.003]

  - Hardening: File Encryption
  - Detection: Dynamic Analysis, Emulated File Analysis, Process Self-Modification Detection, Process Spawn Analysis, File Integrity Monitoring, Endpoint Health Beacon, File Analysis, Process Lineage Analysis
  - Isolation: Content Modification, Content Quarantine, System Call Filtering, Executable Denylisting, Hardware-based Process Isolation, Application-based Process Isolation, Executable Allowlisting, Kernel-based Process Isolation, Local File Permissions, Content Filtering, Remote File Access Mediation
  - Deception: Decoy File
  - Modelling: Network Node Inventory, Physical Link Mapping, Logical Link Mapping
  - Eviction: Host Shutdown, Process Suspension, Process Termination, File Eviction, Host Reboot
  - Restoration: Restore Network Access, Restore File

- Compromise Host Software Binary [T1554]

  - Hardening: Software Update
  - Modelling: Software Inventory, Asset Vulnerability Enumeration
  - Restoration: Restore Software

- Domain Accounts [T1078.002]

  - Hardening: Agent Authentication, Change Default Password
  - Detection: Domain Account Monitoring
  - Isolation: User Account Permissions
  - Modelling: Access Modeling
  - Eviction: Account Locking
  - Restoration: Unlock Account, Restore User Account Access

- Disable or Modify Tools [T1562.001]

  - Detection: Process Self-Modification Detection, Process Spawn Analysis, Process Lineage Analysis, System Daemon Monitoring
  - Isolation: Application-based Process Isolation, Hardware-based Process Isolation, Kernel-based Process Isolation, System Call Filtering
  - Eviction: Process Termination, Process Suspension, Host Shutdown, Host Reboot

- File Deletion [T1070.004]

  - Hardening: File Encryption
  - Detection: File Integrity Monitoring, File Analysis
  - Isolation: Content Quarantine, Content Modification, Local File Permissions, Remote File Access Mediation, Content Filtering
  - Deception: Decoy File
  - Eviction: File Eviction
  - Restoration: Restore File

- Keylogging [T1056.001]

  - Detection: Input Device Analysis
  - Isolation: IO Port Restriction
  - Modelling: Hardware Component Inventory

- Web Portal Capture [T1056.003]

  - Hardening: Software Update
  - Detection: Service Binary Verification
  - Modelling: Software Inventory, Asset Vulnerability Enumeration
  - Restoration: Restore Software

- LSASS Memory [T1003.001]

  - Detection: Process Spawn Analysis, Process Self-Modification Detection, Process Lineage Analysis
  - Isolation: System Call Filtering, Hardware-based Process Isolation, Application-based Process Isolation, Kernel-based Process Isolation, Web Session Access Mediation
  - Eviction: Process Termination, Process Suspension, Host Shutdown, Host Reboot

- NTDS [T1003.003]

  - Hardening: Credential Rotation, File Encryption, Multi-factor Authentication, Credential Hardening
  - Detection: Administrative Network Activity Analysis, Client-server Payload Profiling, Protocol Metadata Anomaly Detection, Network Traffic Community Deviation, Connection Attempt Analysis, Remote Terminal Session Detection, Network Traffic Signature Analysis, Per Host Download-Upload Ratio Analysis, Credential Compromise Scope Analysis, User Geolocation Logon Pattern Analysis, Process Spawn Analysis, Process Self-Modification Detection, File Integrity Monitoring, File Analysis, Process Lineage Analysis, System File Analysis
  - Isolation: Remote File Access Mediation, Content Quarantine, Content Modification, Application-based Process Isolation, Hardware-based Process Isolation, Kernel-based Process Isolation, Credential Transmission Scoping, System Call Filtering, Local File Permissions, Network Traffic Filtering, Content Filtering, Web Session Access Mediation
  - Deception: Decoy User Credential, Decoy File
  - Modelling: Data Inventory
  - Eviction: Credential Revocation, Authentication Cache Invalidation, Process Termination, Process Suspension, Host Shutdown, File Eviction, Host Reboot
  - Restoration: Restore File, Restore Database, Reissue Credential

- System Information Discovery [T1082]

  - Detection: System Call Analysis, Process Spawn Analysis
  - Isolation: Executable Allowlisting, Executable Denylisting, Hardware-based Process Isolation, System Call Filtering
  - Deception: Decoy Environment

- Remote Desktop Protocol [T1021.001]

  - Detection: Network Traffic Community Deviation, Per Host Download-Upload Ratio Analysis, Protocol Metadata Anomaly Detection, Remote Terminal Session Detection, Client-server Payload Profiling, Network Traffic Signature Analysis, Connection Attempt Analysis, User Geolocation Logon Pattern Analysis
  - Isolation: Network Traffic Filtering
  - Eviction: Session Termination

- SMB/Windows Admin Shares [T1021.002]

  - Detection: Client-server Payload Profiling, Connection Attempt Analysis, Network Traffic Community Deviation, Per Host Download-Upload Ratio Analysis, Protocol Metadata Anomaly Detection, Remote Terminal Session Detection, Network Traffic Signature Analysis, User Geolocation Logon Pattern Analysis
  - Isolation: Network Traffic Filtering

- SSH [T1021.004]

  - Detection: Network Traffic Signature Analysis, Protocol Metadata Anomaly Detection, Remote Terminal Session Detection, Client-server Payload Profiling, Per Host Download-Upload Ratio Analysis, Network Traffic Community Deviation, Connection Attempt Analysis, User Geolocation Logon Pattern Analysis
  - Isolation: Network Traffic Filtering
  - Eviction: Session Termination

- DNS [T1071.004]

  - Hardening: File Encryption
  - Detection: Network Traffic Community Deviation, Per Host Download-Upload Ratio Analysis, Network Traffic Signature Analysis, Client-server Payload Profiling, Relay Pattern Analysis, Remote Terminal Session Detection, Certificate Analysis, DNS Traffic Analysis, Protocol Metadata Anomaly Detection, User Geolocation Logon Pattern Analysis, File Integrity Monitoring, File Analysis
  - Isolation: DNS Allowlisting, Network Traffic Filtering, DNS Denylisting, Content Modification, Content Quarantine, Forward Resolution Domain Denylisting, Reverse Resolution IP Denylisting, Local File Permissions, Content Filtering, Outbound Traffic Filtering, Remote File Access Mediation
  - Deception: Decoy File
  - Eviction: File Eviction
  - Restoration: Restore File

- Protocol Tunneling [T1572]

  - Detection: Client-server Payload Profiling, Network Traffic Community Deviation, Per Host Download-Upload Ratio Analysis, Protocol Metadata Anomaly Detection, Relay Pattern Analysis, Remote Terminal Session Detection, Network Traffic Signature Analysis, User Geolocation Logon Pattern Analysis
  - Isolation: Network Traffic Filtering, Outbound Traffic Filtering

- Non-Application Layer Protocol [T1095]

  - Detection: Client-server Payload Profiling, Network Traffic Community Deviation, Per Host Download-Upload Ratio Analysis, Protocol Metadata Anomaly Detection, Relay Pattern Analysis, Remote Terminal Session Detection, Network Traffic Signature Analysis, User Geolocation Logon Pattern Analysis
  - Isolation: Network Traffic Filtering, Outbound Traffic Filtering

- Traffic Signaling [T1205]

  - Detection: Remote Terminal Session Detection, Network Traffic Signature Analysis, Protocol Metadata Anomaly Detection, Client-server Payload Profiling, Per Host Download-Upload Ratio Analysis, Network Traffic Community Deviation, User Geolocation Logon Pattern Analysis
  - Isolation: Network Traffic Filtering

atlas_mapping.summary: ATLAS does not apply to this incident.

items: Modified compcheckresult.cgi to act as a web shell, enabling remote command execution
Modified lastauthserverused.js to harvest VPN credentials entered on the login page
sessionserver.pl and sessionserver.sh were used to bypass integrity checks and hide malicious modifications
VPN configuration and cache data were disguised as .gif / .css files
symantke.com associated with credential exfiltration and command-and-control activity
